title: Today's AI Brief: Māori Text-to-Speech Model Spurns Big Tech’s Values
summary: A Māori text-to-speech model challenges big tech, Waymo's new robotaxi, and the intersection of AI and indigenous values.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Māori Text-to-Speech Model Spurns Big Tech’s Values
imageCaption: Māori Text-to-Speech Model Spurns Big Tech’s Values
imageSourceTitle: Māori Text-to-Speech Model Spurns Big Tech’s Values

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your AI briefing. Today, I'm looking at some interesting developments in the world of artificial intelligence. From a text-to-speech model that's breaking the mold to a new robotaxi hitting the streets, we've got a few stories to explore.

First up, a team in New Zealand has developed a text-to-speech model for the Māori language, one of the country's three official languages. What's remarkable here is that this model is being driven by the Māori community itself, rather than a big tech company. My read is that this is a significant move towards preserving and promoting indigenous languages and cultures in the face of AI's growing influence.

Next, Waymo is launching a new robotaxi, called Ojai, which is manufactured in China. This vehicle will start picking up passengers in Los Angeles, San Francisco, and Phoenix in the coming weeks. I find it interesting that Waymo is expanding its reach with a new, China-made vehicle – it's a reminder that the AI and autonomous vehicle landscapes are increasingly global.

The Māori text-to-speech model caught my attention because it challenges the typical big tech approach to AI development. By controlling their own language model, the Māori community can ensure that their values and culture are preserved, rather than being subject to the priorities of a large corporation. It's a powerful example of AI being used to support and celebrate diverse perspectives.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
